coredata.dc:title: Understanding the adsorption and inhibitive properties of Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots for copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Qiuju
Ge, Ke
Zhang, Shengtao
Tan, Bochuan
coredata.dc:description:
Background This work aimed to explore a way that can effectively alternative traditional and hazardous corrosion inhibitors.
Methods We synthesized a novel N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared by hydrothermal method, elucidated its chemical structure using different spectroscopic techniques, the behavior of NCDs for copper in H2SO4 solution was studied by electrochemical measurement, microscopic surface observation and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Results demonstrate that the inhibitor being studied is a mixed-type, the higher the concentration, the higher the inhibition efficiency, reaching 96.1%. This novel inhibitor could adhere to the copper surface because of its properties of nanomaterials, such as diffusion and agglomeration. Morover, the interaction between Cu2+ and NCDs causes in the formation of a netted anti-corrosion film on the surface of copper.
Significant This work puts forward the corrosion mechanism of NCDs, which can provide powerful guidance for the application of new nanomaterials in metal corrosion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CDs
H2SO4
XPS
Copper

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The main preparation process of NCDs.
(a) the studied NCDs adsorb on the copper substrates, (b) FT-IR spectra of NCDs, and (c) Raman spectra of the NCDs aggregates on the investigated Cu substrates.
(a) XPS spectra of NCDs, and NCDs for (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s.
The characterization of NCDs for TEM image.
(a) OCP-time curves.(b) Polarization curves for the Cu measured in 0.5 M H2SO4 with different concentrations of NCDs.

(a) Equivalent circuits for fitting the EIS with Blank and NCDs, (b) Nyquist diagram of copper electrode recorded in the corrosive medium at 298 K with different concentrations of NCDs, (c-d) Bode diagram for corresponding samples.
At 298, 308, and 318 K, (a-b) polarization curves of a working electrode immersed in corrosive medium with 0 and 100 mg/L NCDs.

The newly polished copper sample surface (a), the copper sample is soaked in 0.5 M H2SO4 with 0 and 100 mg/L NCDs (b, c) for 24 h at 298 K, the corresponding ESEM image of copper and the element distribution of EDS spectrum (d, e).

(a) 3D AFM graph of the freshly polished copper sample surface and corresponding height profile. (b-c) At 298 K, 3D AFM images of copper samples immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions with concentrations of 0 and 100 mg/L NCDs for 12 h, and corresponding copper surface height contour.
Langmuir isotherm is the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy data of NCDs adsorbed at different concentrations on the copper surface.
The adsorption and anti-corrosion mechanism of NCDs on the copper matrix in acid medium is proposed in the diagram.
At 298 K, the polarization parameters of copper Cu samples in the corrosive medium of NCDs with different concentrations.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SD stands for corrosion inhibition efficiency of the three experiments was calculated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: . In corrosive medium with different concentrations of NCDs, the electrochemical parameters of Cu electrode fitting in the EIS results.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SD stands for corrosion inhibition efficiency of the three experiments was calculated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: . The parameters of the polarization test results are obtained from the Tafel curves in 0.5 M H2SO4 without and with 100 mg/L NCDs at different temperatures.

head.ce:title: Understanding the adsorption and inhibitive properties of Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots for copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Chongqing University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400044, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Light Alloy Research Institute, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bLight Alloy Research Institute, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work aimed to explore a way that can effectively alternative traditional and hazardous corrosion inhibitors.

We synthesized a novel N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared by hydrothermal method, elucidated its chemical structure using different spectroscopic techniques, the behavior of NCDs for copper in H2SO4 solution was studied by electrochemical measurement, microscopic surface observation and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Results demonstrate that the inhibitor being studied is a mixed-type, the higher the concentration, the higher the inhibition efficiency, reaching 96.1%. This novel inhibitor could adhere to the copper surface because of its properties of nanomaterials, such as diffusion and agglomeration. Morover, the interaction between Cu2+ and NCDs causes in the formation of a netted anti-corrosion film on the surface of copper.
This work puts forward the corrosion mechanism of NCDs, which can provide powerful guidance for the application of new nanomaterials in metal corrosion.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is widely used in the industrial field on account of its distinguished thermal and electrical conductivity as well as impressive mechanical properties [1–6]. However, the behavior of the corrosiveness to copper could degrade much large equipment performances, which causes grave losses of life and property. For example, China's annual corrosion losses exceed 300 billion dollars or about 3.34% of GDP [7–12]. Sulfuric acid is widely used in the pickling process of metals to remove oxides, corrosion products, and rust. However, during the cleaning process, the metal surface is prone to corrosion. Therefore, dissolving the corrosion inhibitor in an acid solution can prevent metal materials from being corroded [13, 14].

Literature investigations have shown that heteroatoms (such as N, O, P, and S) in organic compounds act as active centers of adsorption sites, promoting interactions between corrosion inhibitors and copper through electron exchange [15–20]. Due to the interaction of π-π and Van der Walls, the bonding strength between the copper surface and acting force could enhance the corrosion-resistant performance of inhibitors [21]. These inhibitors are usually adsorbed on the metal-electrolyte interface through physical adsorption and/or chemical adsorption mechanism [22–25], forming a barrier film to prevent the corrosion medium from diffusing to the metal surface. Therefore, it can be deduced that corrosion inhibitors with stronger absorbability usually also have higher inhibitory capacity. According to this theory, several types of organic corrosion inhibitors have been evaluated in recent decades, which usually contain many active adsorption centers, such as heteroatoms [26, 27] (N, S, O and so on), unsaturated linkage, or polar groups [28–34], Nevertheless, although these inhibitors have excellent corrosion protection capability, most of the organic corrosion inhibitors are still toxic. With the improvement of people's environmental protection consciousness, it's of important meanings to find low- or nontoxic to replace those toxic corrosion inhibitors. In addition, the poor water-solubility of most organic corrosion inhibitors seriously limits their wide application [6]. So it is meaningful to explore environmentally friendly, water-soluble, and efficient corrosion inhibitors [32].

In recent years, due to the excellent water solubility, photoluminescence, and hypotonicity of CDs, carbon dots have attracted more and more attention [35-38]. However, the research on carbon points mainly focuses on the imaging ability of multicolor cells, and hardly involves the field of corrosion. Ye et al. selected functionalized carbon dots (CDs) obtained from citric acid derivatives as intercalating agents to modify graphene, and then dispersed them in an epoxy matrix to prepare CDs-modified graphene/epoxy (CDs-G). / EP) coating, the CD can greatly enhance the dispersion and interface compatibility of graphene [26]. Ye et al. obtained green N-doped carbon dot inhibitors (NCDs) from the reaction between citric acid and l-histidine, which can effectively protect Q235 from corrosion in HCl. The inhibitory efficiency of NCDs at 100 mg L 1 exceeds 90% [27]. Cui et al. firstly proposed that NCDs obtained by doping N into a carbon source can effectively protect Fe from corrosion in 1 M HCl solution. It is feasible that N and other heteroatom doping can enhance the adsorption capacity of corrosion inhibitors and make it have excellent inhibition performance [29]. Nevertheless, the anti-corrosion mechanism of NCDs is still unclear, and most NCDs are synthesized by small molecules of organic matter, but there are few reports on polymer synthesized carbon dots as corrosion inhibitors.

Therefore, the purpose of this work is to find the potential of newly synthesized NCDs as the copper inhibitor in 0.5 M H2SO4 and provide insights for exploring the corresponding corrosion anti-corrosion mechanism. The newly obtained NCDs have been analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), UV-3600 spectroscopy (UV), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Then, the suppression behavior of the obtained NCDs was systematically studied by the techniques of potential polarization, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, and surface analysis techniques. In addition, related corrosion protection mechanisms are proposed in detail.

Citric Acid Monohydrate (≥99.5 wt%), AR-grade sulfuric acid)98 wt%(, ethyl alcohol and acetone were purchased from a local supplier. Ethyleneimine polymer (MW=600, 99 wt%) was purchased from Meryer (Shanghai) Chemical Technology Co, Ltd. Dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da) was purchased from the United States. Ultra-pure water (18.25 MΩcm) was used throughout the experiment.

According to Fig 1 , NCDs were obtained by the hydrothermal method. Briefly, preparation of NCDs: Firstly, the 1.0 g citric acid and 0.6 g polyethyleneimine branched (PEI) were dissolved in 30 mL ultra-pure water and oscillated by ultrasound until completely dissolved. Secondly, the solution was transferred to a teflon-sealed autoclave lined with 50 mL and heated for 16 h at 180 °C, and all the obtained NCDs solution was passed through the 0.45 μm membrane to remove the larger part and using a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) in ultrapure water for 24 h. Finally, the solid powder is obtained by freeze drying [39, 40].

The Structure of NCDs was analyzed by FT-IR (Nicolet iS50, America), Raman and XPS (ESCALAB250Xi, Al Kα Xray source), XRD and UV-3600 spectrometer. Obtain the morphology and distribution results of NCDs by DTEM (FEI Talos F200S G2). FT-IR spectrum was collected from 500 to 4000 cm−1 using KBr tablets, The Raman spectrum was measured with a LabRAM HR Evolution spectrophotometer. XPS spectroscopy was collected spectrometer using a full-range.

The working electrode was made of cubic copper (99.999% purity) and sealed with epoxy resin. The electrode surface area measured by electrochemical measurements was 1 cm2, and the working electrode surface was polished with emery paper (400, 800, 1200, 2000 grit, sequential), washed with ultrapure water (18.25 MΩcm), cleaned with ethyl alcohol and acetone by an ultrasonic for 2 min, dried under cold airflow, soaking the electrode in the corrosive solution instantly. All tests were conducted in 0.5 M H2SO4, which is a compound from 98 wt% H2SO4 and ultrapure water. The concentrations of NCDs range from 0.2 to 100 mg/L. 0.5 M H2SO4 solution without NCDs is treated as blank. In particular, the NCDs solution prepared in advance was used in each experiment.

Electrochemical tests were performed under the unstirred condition at the CHI 760E Electrochemical workstation equipped with a three-electrode system. The three-electrode system was composed of the platinum plane (auxiliary electrode), the saturated calomel electrode (reference electrode) and the copper electrode (working electrode). Firstly, the working electrode was immersed with open potential (EOCP) in the electrolyte for 2400s to ensure the stability of the system. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were then performed on EOCP at a sinusoidal potential signal of 5 mV in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz. Finally, Zsimpwin software was used to analyze EIS data. Compared with EOCP, the potentiometric polarization measurement is performed at 2 mV s − 1 in the potential range of −250 mV to +250 mV. Three experiments were conducted under the same conditions for each test to ensure good reproducibility.

AFM and ESEM (Thermo scientific Quattro S) were used to observe the surface morphology of Cu soaked in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with 0 and 100 mg/L NCDs at 298 K. The EDS detector combined with ESEM records the corrosion composition on the surface of copper samples.

FT-IR spectroscopy is used to determine the functional group information of NCDs. Analyze Fig. 2 (a-b) to confirm their conversion. In the FT-IR spectrum of NCDs the band at 3272 cm−1 was OH and NH groups, these hydrophilicity functional groups could explain the stability and good solubility of the carbon dots in an aqueous system. Absorption peaks at 2836 cm−1 were attributed to the CH bond, the peaks at 1694 cm−1 and 1647cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of C=O and -CONH-, respectively [41]. The peaks at 1538, 1437 cm−1 and 1296 cm−1 are due to the tensile vibration of C=N, CN and CO, respectively. Compared to Fig. 2b, Fig. 2a suggests that the characteristic peak of the C=N (1538 cm−1), and the CN (1437 cm−1) bonding can not be discovered. The results indicate that NCDs, and Cu ions in H2SO4 solution may form the chemical chelating bond [42, 43].

The Raman spectra of the copper sample after immersing in sulfuric acid solution with NCDs for 12 h are shown in Fig. 2c. A broad and obvious NCDs peak is observed in Fig. 2c, ranging from 1250 to 1650 cm−1, which may correspond to the G and D bands, indicating the stretching vibrations generated by the hybridization of sp2 and sp3, respectively. In addition, no single chemical bond or double bond extension is found between N and C in the range of 960–1280 cm−1, while the N: Cu band was found in the range of 3900–570 cm−1 [44, 45]. The results further reflect the chemical interaction between the studied NCDs, and the surface of the Cu sample in the H2SO4 solution.

The bonding information of the samples was obtained by XPS measurement and the full XPS spectrum of NCD was shown in Fig. 3 a. According to the XPS spectrogram, the NCDs mainly contain three elements, corresponding to C, N, and O, respectively. C 1 s spectrum is shown in Fig. 3b, including three peaks, which are locating at 284.7, 285.7, and 287.5 eV, related to C = C, CO/CN, and C = O bonds respectively. The peak at 284.7 eV indicates the presence of C atom [32, 46]. In terms of N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3c), the nitrogen peaks at 399.3 and 400.1 eV are associated with the form of CN-C and (C)3 N, respectively, which proves that the N atom has been doped into the CDs [32]. For the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3d), the peak at a binding energy of 531 and 531.9 eV is related to C=O and COH/CO-C, respectively. Thus, the NCDs have been successfully obtained as expected [32, 47, 48].

The XRD diagram of NCDs (Fig. 4 a) shows a sharp diffraction peak with a center of 2θ at approximately 20.3°, as can be seen from the XRD peak shape, the synthesized NCDs are crystalline state. With the corresponding interlayer spacing of around 0.4436 nm, compared with the graphite 002 crystal plane (0.34 nm), NCDs have a wider interplanar spacing, which may be caused by the doping of heteroatoms such as N, which increases the repulsive force between the molecules [47]. Fig. 4b is the UV-3600 spectrum of NCDs, including two apparent adsorption bands, influenced by double bond, conjugate and solitary electron, the adsorption bands at 220 and 450 nm belong to the C=O bond π-π* electron transition and the C=N bond n-π* electron transition, respectively [46, 49].

According to Fig. 5 , the surface morphology and size distribution of the NCDs were observed by TEM. The sample dispersed to the carbon film for observation, high-resolution image of TEM clearly shows that the synthesized NCDs has an evenly spherical structure with a size distribution between 10 and 20 nm. The synthesized NCDs is a little larger than ordinary CDs, which may be caused by the random polymerization and cleavage reaction of the synthesized nanomaterials, the results of TEM indicate that NCDs have been successfully achieved.

It is essential to observe the open circuit potential (OCP) shift in electrolyte liquid for understanding adsorption process characters of NCDs to copper, therefore, gaining stable potential before the electrochemical performance test. Fig. 6 a shows the OCP-time curves of NCDs with different concentrations in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at 298 K. Under all concentration conditions, OCP remained stable for more than 1000 s. In conclusion, Compared with the blank solution(0.5 M H2SO4), all the Eocp curves move in the negative direction. For the lower concentration solution, Eocp first decreases and then increases until it stabilizes. Initially, the negative shift of potential is related to the dissolution of the oxide layer on the copper surface. The fluctuation time of the negative shift of potential after adding NCDs to the sulfuric acid solution was related to the adhesion of NCDs. Then, there will be a significant positive offset until the potential stabilizes. However, under the condition of higher corrosion inhibitor concentration, there will be a significant positive shift until the potential is stable.

The potentiodynamic polarization curves were tested to study the characteristics of the inhibitor. Fig. 6b manifests the polarization curves of the working electrode after soaking in NCDs solutions of different concentrations for 2400 s at 298 K. Table 1 summarizes the relevant data, including the corrosion current density (icorr ), corrosion potential (Ecorr ), anode slope (βa ), cathode Tafel slope (βc ), and suppression efficiency (η) are derived from the polarization curve by classical extrapolation. The corresponding η value is calculated as following [6, 23, 24, 32, 46]:
(1)
n
( % )
=
i
c o r r , 0
−
i
c o r r
i
c o r r , 0
× 100 %

Where icorr,
0 and icorr represent the current density of the unprotected and protected copper electrodes, respectively. In terms of Fig. 6b, adding NCDs to the corrosion solution will cause the corrosion potential (Ecorr ) to shift negatively. In addition, compared with blank solution, the icorr of the anode and cathode is greatly reduced, and the cathode branch of the polarization curve of the sulfuric acid solution with NCDs moved to a lower current density, while the anode branch hardly changed. In the case of Table 1, the ratio of the Ecorr value between the blank and the solution with NCDs is less than 85 mV. Various phenomena indicate that NCDs may be considered as a moderate cathodic inhibitor, which mainly limits the cathodic reaction. In particular, the polarization curves after adding NCDs to 0.5 M H2SO4 were parallel to the blank solution. What is more, the slope of anode and cathode remains almost the same after the addition of NCDs. This indicates that the introduction of NCDs into the corrosion solution will not affect the mechanism of the anode (copper dissolution) and cathode reaction (oxygen reduction). Furthermore, the suppression effect mainly lies in reducing the current density. Moreover, the icorr value decreases significantly as the concentration of NCDs increases according to data compiled by Table 1, resulting in an increase in the η value. The corrosion inhibition efficiency ranges from 69.6% at 0.2 mg/L to 87.6% at 100 mg/L, which may be attributed to the accumulation of NCDs of different sizes on the copper substrate, further blocking the active sites of the copper electrode surface. Thus, it can be inferred that the copper substrate is covered with a stable NCDs adsorption film, which can effectively inhibit Cu corrosion in the sulfuric medium.

EIS technology is applied to explore the anti-corrosion inhibition effect and anti-corrosion mechanism of synthesized NCDs. Under the same immersion time (2400 s) and temperature (298 K), Fig. 7 b and Fig. 7 (c-d) show the Nyquist and Bode diagrams of working electrodes in NCDs solution of different concentrations, respectively. All the frequency spectra display a concave semi-circular of capacitance in the high-frequency range, while there is a warped arc in the low-frequency area. In the high-frequency region, the capacitive loop determined by the double-layer capacitance is a charge transfer process, which is related to the interface reaction, especially for the reaction of copper at the solid-liquid /protective film interface. Moreover, the tailed straight line represents the Warburg impedance, Warburg impedance ascribed to the mass transfer and diffusion process on the electrode surface Therefore, the dissolution process of copper is affected by the process of charge transfer and diffusion. According to the electromotive force series, the electromotive force of copper is higher than H+. Thus, the cathodic reaction will cause the release of hydrogen and the dissolution of the metal, which is the reduction of oxygen (the detailed equation shown in the anti-corrosion mechanism). After adding NCDs to the corrosive medium, the diameter of the semicircle in the high-frequency range increases with the increase of the concentration, indicating that NCDs can form an adsorption film on copper, thereby inhibiting the charge transfer process. In addition, the samples after adding NCDs have the same shape as the blank solution curve, which indicates that the presence of NCDs can increase impedance, but will not change other electrochemical properties. It can be seen from the Bode diagram of Fig. 7c and d that as the content of NCDs increases, the impedance modulus and the frequency range of the maximum phase angle in the entire frequency range also increase. These results also demonstrate that NCD molecules adsorb on the copper, which effectively restrains the corrosion of Cu by sulfuric acid solution. For analysis and study of the electrochemical parameters of working electrode surface impedance spectrum, the EIS data of the blank solution and NCDs were fitted by the equivalent circuit in Fig. 7a. The fitted results indicate that the values of chi-square (χ2) are less than 10−2. Theoretically, the lower value of χ2 indicates that the fitted data agree well with the experimental results.

In the electrical equivalent circuit, due to various physical phenomena (such as surface roughness, adsorption, porous layer formation, etc.), CPE is used as a constant phase element in place of an ideal capacitor, Rs is the solution resistance, Rp stands for the polarization resistance, Rf corresponds to the film resistance of corrosion matters, CPEf reflects the capacitance response of corrosion products film. CPEdl represents the double layer capacitor related to constant phase angle element, ndl is the modulus of the dispersion effect related to the electrode and the solution, Rct represents the resistance to charge transfer in corrosion reactions, W corresponds to Warburg resistance, and is caused by the diffusion of corrosion products on the metal surface [50], The CPE impedance is calculated via the following equation:
(2)
Z
C P E
=
1
Y 0
( j ω )
n
(3)
C =
Y 0
( ω )
n − 1
=
Y 0
( 2 π
f
z
i m − M a x
)
n − 1

Where Y0 represents the value of CPE, j is the imaginary unit, ω corresponds to the angular frequency, and n represents the deviation index. The constant phase angle element CPE can be judged according to the value of n, the exponent n (−1 ≤ n ≤ 1) is related to the distribution of the time constant. When n=−1, 0, 0.5, and 1, the constant phase components correspond to the inductive reactance, resistance, Warburg impedance, and capacitance, respectively.

Table 2 lists the values of the parameters of the corresponding fitting results. The inhibition efficiency of NCDs for copper in the corrosive medium is calculated by the polarization resistance Rp as following formulas (5), where Rp and Rp,
0 represent the sum of Rct and Rf , the values of η.
(4)
R P
=
R
c t
+
R f
(5)
η
( % )
=
R P
−
R
P , 0
R P

According to table 3 , it is clear that the values of Rct, Rf and Rp increase, while Cdl and Cf show a downward trend overall. Therefore, it can be inferred that NCD molecules adsorb on the Cu substrate and form an adsorption film to separate the corrosive media from the copper, thereby slowing down the dissolution rate of copper. As a result, the value of η increased with the increase of NCDs concentration, from 78.8% at 298 K at 0.2 mg/L to 96.1% at 100 mg/L. The excellent inhibition performance is inconsistent with the results obtained by the potential dynamic polarization measurement. In conclusion, the NCDs protective film on the Cu surface effectively protect Cu from corrosion by the acid medium.

Polarization measurement studied the corrosion inhibition performance of NCDs at 298, 308 and 318 K. Fig. 8 shows that the polarization curves of working electrode soaked in corrosive medium, which containing 0 and 100 mg/L NCDs at different temperatures. Table 3 displays the relevant parameters in the experiment. As the temperature increases, the curve shifts to a higher corrosion current density in both the blank solution and inhibited solution, which leads to an increase in the icorr value. These results indicate that the corrosion rate of Cu accelerates with the increase of temperature, which is due to the thermal movement of activated nanoparticles, which makes the adsorption layer unstable and promotes the corrosion process. Compared with the blank solution, when 100 mg/L NCDs were added at each temperature, the icorr value was significantly reduced, which means that NCDs greatly inhibited the corrosion process of Cu. However, the protection strength of NCDs adsorption film was weakened by temperature increase, so the value of η decreased from 87.6% at 298 K to 58.2% at 318 K. Fortunately, although temperature rise will accelerate corrosion, NCDs still shows excellent inhibition performance at 308 K.

Fig. 9 shows E-SEM images and corresponding EDS spectra of copper samples immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium containing 0 and 100 mg/L NCDs at 298 K. All the copper samples tested surface were polished with emery paper (2000, 3000, 5000, 7000 grit, sequential), washed with ultrapure water (18.25 MΩcm), Compared with the new polishing copper block (Fig. 9a), another copper block was immediately soaking in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution for 24 h after polishing (Fig. 9b). According to Fig. 9, the surface of the copper sample soaked in the corrosive medium was seriously damaged with a large number of holes and cracks, indicating that sulfuric acid had a strong corrosive effect on copper. However, once the NCDs were added to the corrosive medium, the corrosion degree of the copper was significantly inhibited, and the surface morphology of the copper sample was improved, with only a small amount of polishing and corrosion traces, as shown in Fig. 9c. EDS was used to analyze the elemental composition of the copper sample surface. Compared with the copper sample without corrosion inhibitor protection (Fig. 9c), the EDS spectra of the copper sample with corrosion inhibitor protection were shown in Fig. 9e. The increase of C and N peaks express that an adsorption film was formed on the copper surface, and which was composed of elements C, N, and so on. The suppression layer can effectively protect Cu from being eroded by H2SO4, thus reducing the content of O and S on the surface of the copper sample.

The nanostructures, sample surface roughness, height distribution, and various physical and chemical properties of interfaces during metal corrosion can be characterized by AFM. Fig. 10 is AFM images of NCDs, it shows the surface roughness of freshly the polished copper and copper samples soaked in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing 0 and 100 mg/L for 12 h at 298 K. The surface treatment method of all copper samples is the same as the SEM test. Compared with the surface of the freshly polished Cu (Fig. 10a), the surface of the copper sample soaked in the blank solution (0.5 M H2SO4) has more pits and porosity. The corrosion height profile of the copper sample surface shows a large fluctuation of about 120 nm (Fig.10a). While the inhibitor adds into the corrosive solution (Fig. 7c), the surface of the copper sample (Fig. 10c) is relatively flat and small fluctuations in the height within 50 nm can be noticed, which proves that the NCDs are significant slow down the corrosion process of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Moreover, by adding NCDs, the average roughness (Ra) was reduced from 47.44 nm (No NCDs) to 15.263 nm. These findings further confirm that the presence of NCDs can effectively slow down the corrosion of copper in H2SO4.

Generally, adsorption isotherms can be used to clarify the adsorption properties of inhibitors, which obtain some important information about the interaction of corrosion inhibitors with metal surfaces [51]. The EIS results have a good linear relationship for the Langmuir isotherm model, which is given and calculated as follows [52]:
(6)
θ
1 − θ
=
K
a d s
c

Where
θ
(
n
/
100
)
,
K
a d s
and C represent surface coverage, adsorption equilibrium constant, and concentration of NCDs, respectively. Fig. 11 display that the EIS data fitting results show an excellent linear relationship with an intercept about 1/Kads . The standard adsorption free energy G0 adss is associate with the adsorption equilibrium constant as follows [53]:
(7)
K
a d s
=
1
C
H 2
o
exp
(
−
Δ
G
a d s
0
R T
)
where C is approximately 1 g/mL, R and T are representative of the universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), the experiment temperature (K), respectively [54, 55]. The value of ΔG° ads can determine the character of the adsorption process [56, 57]. When the value of ΔG° ads is greater than −20 kJ mol−1, the main process involves physical adsorption, which occurs through electrostatic interaction [58]; if it is less than −40 kJ mol−1. it mainly involves chemical adsorption, which is caused by the transfer of electrons from the inhibitor molecule to the metal [6, 59-63]; when ΔG° ads is between −20 and −40 kJ mol−1, both physical adsorption and chemical adsorption can occur [64].

From Fig. 11, ΔG° ads is low to −33.39 kJ/mol, thus the ΔG° ads values are between −20 and −40 kJ mol−1, which indicats that physical adsorption and chemical adsorption processes have occurred, the value of Kads is high to 707.8 (L/g), it shows that the NCDs have a strong adsorption force on the copper surface, and owning high inhibitive ability [65, 66].

In the present study, NCDs species as a corrosion inhibitor and firstly applied to protect Cu in H2SO4 solution. For the sake of understanding the interaction between Cu and NCDs, it is necessary to clarify the anti-corrosion mechanism of synthesized NCDs on the Cu surface.

The main anti-corrosion mechanism of most organic inhibitors is adsorption and coordination [67]. The evidence that electrochemical performance and micromorphology strongly reveal the adsorption film has formed on the Cu substrate in the sulfuric acid medium, and the cathodic oxygen reduction reaction in sulfuric acid solution can be as follows [68]:
O 2
+ 4
H +
+ 4
e −
→
H 2
O

According to reports, the anodic dissolution process of Cu in H2SO4 solution is carried out in the following steps [9, 69]:
C u ↔ C
u
a d s
+
+
e −
( f a s t )
C
u +
↔ C
u 2
+
e −
( slow )
According to the value of ΔG° ads in this work, the interaction of NCDs on the Cu matrix in H2SO4 medium is mixed adsorption, including physical, and chemical adsorption.

Various Cu chelate complexes are easy to coordinate with hydroxyl, carboxyl and heterocyclic. NCDs can be used as coordination sites, which contain carbon chains and a variety of functional groups [70]. Once the NCDs are adsorbed on the surface of the copper sample, complex reactions will occur between Cu2+ and NCDs, which can be demonstrated by FT-IR (Fig. 2), XPS (Fig. 3), and UV (Fig. 4). According to the SEM results (Fig. 6), plenty of NCDs and Cu2+ combine to form a protective layer with a network structure, while the nanoparticles formed by the aggregation of NCDs act as supporting points, and Cu2+ complexes act as connecting lines. Fig. 12 is the schematic diagram of the mechanism of NCDs inhibiting copper corrosion in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution.

The process of NCDs protecting copper has the following aspects. Firstly, due to diffusion, NCDs are close to the metal/solution interface, and part of the NCD is adsorbed on the copper surface through chemical and physical mixing. Because the sizes of these NCDs is larger than normal N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), then due to the agglomeration effect, the NCDs near the interface migrate to the copper surface and aggregates to form larger nanoparticles. Cu2+-N-CDs chelate when the dissolved copper escapes from the interface into the corrosive medium, especially for multiple coordination sites of Cu2+ and NCDs. Multiple branches of NCDs nanoparticles are connected to the copper surface to form a network covering the protective film, which not only effectively isolates the direct contact between sulfuric acid and the copper surface, but also inhibits the electrochemical reaction between the cathode and the anode.

According to experimental and theoretical research, the results obtained can be inferred:
•
The prepared NCDs was succeeded synthesised, and the nanoparticle size is evenly distributed in 10–20 nm, the information was given by FT-IR and XPS that illustrate the NCDs contain abundant N-groups.
•
The results from Tafel suggest that NCDs is a moderate cathodic-type inhibitor.
•
The EIS results were consistent with those obtained through potentiometric dynamic polarization measurements, inferring that NCDs can effectively protect copper in sulfuric medium, and the anti-corrosion efficiency reaches 96.1% at 100 mg L − 1.
•
The adsorption process of NCDs on Cu surface follows the Langmuir adsorption model, and it involves chemical adsorption and physical adsorption.
•
Although the corrosion rate of the copper sample increases with the increase of temperature, NCDs still have excellent inhibition performance at 298–308 K.
•
According to the results of FT-IR, Raman, AFM, SEM, and EDS, NCDs can chelate with Cu2+, the protective film on the surface of the copper sample is composed of the agglomeration of NCDs and the complex with Cu2+.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878029, and 21676035). Sail plan of Guangdong, China (No. 2015YT02D025).
